About Me

Student Photographer · Leander ISD

Derek Wright

I'm a student photographer with a deep passion for portrait and street-style photography. My work explores human connection, authenticity, and the way light shapes the world around us — shot primarily on a Sony a6400.

10+
Photo challenges
2
Years shooting
2
Disciplines

Photography found me during my sophomore year and quickly became more than a class — it became a language. I shoot primarily with a Sony a6400 paired with an 18–135mm lens, giving me flexibility to move between tight environmental portraits and wider street scenes without missing a beat.

My sustained investigation explores urban isolation and infrastructure — power lines, empty roads, graffiti-covered underpasses, crows on wires. Each photo title references a song, lyric, or album, adding a layer of meaning to what might otherwise seem like overlooked subjects.

Beyond digital, I've explored the tactile side of photography through darkroom printing and cyanotype work. There's something irreplaceable about an analog print — the grain, the unpredictability, the physicality. Both worlds inform each other in my practice.

I'm currently in Photography II, continuing to refine my eye and push my technical skills. My goal is to eventually shoot editorial and documentary-style portraiture, telling the stories of people and places through genuine, unposed moments.

Sustained Investigation

Isolation, infrastructure, and the overlooked — each title references a song, lyric, or album

SI Statement

I adopt a minimalist approach, specifically focusing on isolating subjects within vast environments to create a powerful sense of solitude and focus. I enhance the emotional resonance by utilizing significant negative space — expansive skies, large landscapes, or architectural textures — allowing the subject to stand out and providing a unique creativity that engages the viewer.

Artist Statement

"Creativity is driven further when we stop paying attention to the beautiful and start looking for the boring."

I have always been drawn to the quiet resonance of isolated subjects. For me, temporary isolation is not about loneliness, but about the clarity that enhances an artist's creative functions. By intentionally focusing on a single point within a vast or empty space, I am forced to engage more deeply with the overlooked features of an urban landscape.
